---
title: "Final Portfolio + Reflection"
subtitle: "Bringing the term's work together"
toc: true
---

> **Pairs with:** Weeks 13–15. The Week 13 portfolio-workflow
> conference is the structured closing checkpoint; the portfolio and
> the 1–2 page reflection are due in Week 15 through Blackboard.

## Goal

Assemble the small portfolio you have built across the term into a
**single, polished, well-organized collection**, and pair it with a
**short written reflection**.

This is the course's capstone. There are no new technical skills to
learn — the work is **curation, polish, and reflection**.

## What is required

- A **portfolio** — a small, well-organized collection of the term's
  artifacts (see "What to include" below).
- A **1–2 page final reflection** — a rendered Quarto document.

There is **no required final demo** and **no required Week 15
conference**. The **Week 13 portfolio-workflow conference** is the
structured closing checkpoint — that is where we walk through your
portfolio organization, your AI Use Notes, and your final-reflection
plan together before final assembly.

## Portfolio location — your choice

The portfolio may live as:

- a **local folder** on your computer,
- a **private repository** (GitHub, GitLab, etc.), or
- a **public repository**, if and only if you specifically choose to
  make your work public.

**Portfolio organization is required; public posting is optional.**
Public GitHub is never required. Most students submit a zipped local
folder; a few choose a public repo. Either way, **Blackboard is the
submission inbox**.

## What to include

A complete portfolio contains:

| Component | What it is |
|-----------|-----------|
| **README** | A short index that says who you are, what is in the folder, and how to render anything. |
| **A selection of weekly homework** | 6–8 of your stronger weekly Quarto documents. You do not need every one. |
| **LaTeX Project** | The PDF and source. |
| **R Project** | The Quarto source and rendered PDF, plus any data. |
| **AI module artifacts** | Your AI debugging audit (HW 11) and AI Use Reflection (HW 12). |
| **(Optional) Bridge mini-report** | If you did HW 13, include it. |
| **Final reflection** | 1–2 pages — see below. |

## README expectations

A short Markdown file at the root of your portfolio. Something like:

```markdown
# math-software-portfolio — YOUR NAME

A small portfolio of mathematical software work from MATH 12362.

## Contents

- `hw01/` — first Quarto document, setup
- `hw02/` — math in LaTeX
- ...
- `latex-project/` — LaTeX paper replication
- `r-project/` — R data or simulation report
- `ai-module/` — debugging audit and reflection
- `reflection.pdf` — final reflection

## How to render

Each folder contains a `.qmd` (or `.tex`) source. From a folder, run
`quarto render <file>.qmd` (or `pdflatex` for `.tex` sources).
```

The README is not graded for prose quality. It is graded for whether
**someone unfamiliar with your work can navigate the folder**.

## Final reflection

A 1–2 page Quarto document called `reflection.qmd` (rendered to PDF).

Address these four questions, briefly and honestly:

1. **What did you build?** A paragraph summarizing the portfolio.
2. **What did you learn about each pillar** (LaTeX, R, technical
   workflow, generative AI literacy) that you did not know in Week 1?
3. **Where did AI help, and where did it hurt?** Concrete examples.
   This connects to the **verification** line in your AI Use Notes:
   what did you catch, and what almost slipped through?
4. **What would you do differently next term?** Tool-wise,
   skill-wise, and workflow-wise.

This is reflective writing, not a brag sheet. Honest self-assessment
is worth more than overclaiming. Include a short **AI Use Note** in
the reflection (Tool / Purpose / Verification).

## Grading

This piece is evaluated across the course's four categories, with
weight on **technical workflow** (organization, reproducibility,
README clarity), **mathematical writing** (the reflection itself), and
**generative AI literacy** (the AI Use Note and the AI portion of your
reflection).

## AI use

You are welcome to use AI to help organize, polish, or draft portions
of the reflection. The same rules apply: include an **AI Use Note**
in the reflection (Tool / Purpose / Verification), and do not submit
prose you cannot explain.
